content:
Continuing from the last part, the priest does not only place the holy portions into the Nazirite’s hands. Numbers 6 says he also lifts them before Yahweh as a wave offering. This means the Nazirite is not simply receiving something for private possession. He is receiving something in Yahweh’s presence, and then those same gifts are lifted up in worship. This is a powerful order: Yahweh gives, and then what Yahweh gives is presented back to Him in thanksgiving.

This matches a deep pattern across Scripture. Everything we have comes from God. Even our strength to endure is from Him. Even our victory over sin is through His help. So when Yahweh rewards, the reward itself becomes a reason to worship. Revelation shows this clearly when the elders cast their crowns before the throne (Revelation 4:10). They do not throw them away in shame. They lay them down in worship. It is their way of saying, “All honor came from You.” In the Nazirite ceremony, the priest places holy portions into the hands of the one who finished and then lifts them before Yahweh. The lesson is the same: reward does not create pride. Reward creates worship.

Now the shoulder portion adds more meaning. The shoulder is linked with strength, bearing, and government. Isaiah says of Messiah, “the government shall be upon His shoulder” (Isaiah 9:6). So when the priest places the shoulder in the Nazirite’s hands, and then waves it before Yahweh, it is like Yahweh is teaching that strength and authority are not for self-glory. They are for holy service. In the resurrection hope, the saints are given priesthood and reigning, but it is not for domination. It is for righteous service under Christ. Revelation speaks of thrones and judgment being committed, and it also says those raised are priests and reign with Christ (Revelation 20:4-6). Paul says the saints will judge the world and even angels (1 Corinthians 6:2-3). This is a heavy trust, and it is holy. That is why it is waved before Yahweh. It shows that authority belongs to Yahweh first and remains under Yahweh forever.

The unleavened cake and wafer also deepen this. Unleavened bread speaks of sincerity and truth (1 Corinthians 5:7-8). It speaks of a life without corruption. When the priest waves the unleavened portion before God, it is like presenting purity back to the Holy One. It is saying that the final state of Yahweh’s people is clean, not mixed. This fits the resurrection promises. Paul says we are raised in incorruption (1 Corinthians 15:42). He says mortality puts on immortality
(1 Corinthians 15:52-54). Peter says our inheritance is incorruptible and undefiled (1 Peter 1:4). So the waved unleavened bread can picture the overcomer standing before Yahweh in a state where corruption will never return.

This also teaches a practical truth for today. If the future reward is waved before Yahweh, then the present obedience must also be lived before Yahweh.
Many times we live for human eyes. We obey when others watch. We speak gently when others listen. But Yahweh is calling for a life that is always “before Him.” The Nazirite finished the vow at the tabernacle entrance. The gifts were placed into his hands there. And the waving happened there. It was public worship before Yahweh. In the same way, our daily life is meant to be lived before Yahweh, not before men. Christ taught that the Father sees in secret (Matthew 6:4-6). Paul said we must do all things as unto the Yahweh (Colossians 3:23). This is how we train for the end: living now as those who will one day stand before God.

The waving also teaches that reward is not the final goal by itself. God is the goal. The gifts do not replace the Giver. They return to the Giver in thanksgiving. Even in resurrection glory, worship remains. Even in shared honor, humility remains. Even in priesthood and reigning, submission remains. That is why the priest waves the portions. It is like saying: “These gifts are holy, and they belong to God. The one who receives must also worship.”

This should comfort the heart. Some fear that thinking about reward will create pride. God’s pattern prevents that. Reward is placed into the hands, and then it is lifted before God. The very act of receiving becomes an act of worship.
It teaches that all is from grace. It teaches that even the crown is safe when it is laid before God. It teaches that the end of the race is not self-exaltation, but God’s glory.

So the whole picture becomes clear. First the vow is completed. Then the temporary sign is laid down. Then the priest places holy portions into the hands. Then those portions are waved before God. Spiritually, first the earthly race is finished. Then what was temporary ends. Then Christ our Priest shares holy reward. Then we stand before God in worship, holding what was given, presenting it back, confessing that all honor belongs to Him. This is the safest and sweetest end: acceptance with worship.

title: Prayer
text:
My God, keep our hearts humble, so reward never becomes pride.
Teach us to live before Your eyes now, as those who will stand before You later.
Make our obedience sincere and unleavened, full of truth and free from hypocrisy.
Strengthen our endurance so we finish the course, and keep our hope fixed on the resurrection.
When You place holy things into our hands, teach us to lift them back to You in worship.
May all glory return to You, and may Christ’s life be seen in us until the end.
In Christ name, amen.
